title: Buying a nucleus hive: what to check before you collect
subtitle: The questions to ask and the inspection that matters.
excerpt: A nucleus colony is the standard starting point for a new beekeeper. Most nucs are healthy and well-prepared, but a few basics protect you from buying disease, a failing queen, or a colony that barely has the numbers to build up through summer. Knowing what to look for before you load the nuc into the car makes all the difference.
sourceNotes: BBKA guide to buying bees (2022), bbka.org.uk. NBU BeeBase disease information, nationalbeeunit.com. BBKA recommended conditions of sale for honeybee colonies.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small starter colony of four to six frames, typically containing a mated laying queen, brood in all stages, stores of honey and pollen, and enough nurse and forager bees to function independently.

A notifiable bacterial disease of honeybee brood. American foulbrood (Paenibacillus larvae) is the most serious; European foulbrood (Melissococcus plutonius) is less severe but both require reporting to the NBU.

content.text: bought from a reputable local beekeeper is the best starting point for most UK beekeepers. Local bees are adapted to local conditions, local suppliers know the nuc's history, and the sale usually comes with informal ongoing support. Buying at a distance, or from an unknown internet seller, carries higher risk.
Ask the seller directly: Is the colony registered on BeeBase? What was the varroa count at last treatment? When was the queen reared? Has the colony been treated for
or had any disease history? A seller who answers these questions readily and confidently is usually the right seller. A seller who cannot or will not answer them is a warning sign.
Most sellers will show you the nuc open before closing it for transport. Suit up and look for:

A laying queen, eggs standing upright in cells confirm she has been laying within the last three days. Spot the queen if you can; check that she is moving easily and does not look damaged or undersized.

Good brood pattern, solid cappings with few gaps, uniform mid-brown colour, smelling of warm bread. Sunken or greasy-looking cappings, a distinctive sour smell, or larvae that are discoloured yellow or brown are signs of disease.

Sufficient numbers, four or five frames well covered with bees and at least two frames of capped brood. A nuc sold in April with only one frame of brood and sparse coverage will struggle to build before the first honey flow.
Some stores, at least one frame showing capped honey around the brood area. A nuc with no food stores needs immediate feeding.

The seller will seal the entrance before you leave. Transport the nuc on the floor of the car, not in the boot, and if possible with a window slightly open on a warm day. A sealed box in a hot car kills bees quickly. At home, place the nuc on its permanent stand before opening the entrance, bees orientate to the spot where they first fly, not to the box they arrived in.

Open the nuc entrance at dusk on the day of arrival or early the following morning. Leave the colony undisturbed for five to seven days before the first inspection. They need time to orientate, build up stores, and settle. An early inspection on a colony that is already stressed from transport achieves little and risks losing the queen.
